summary: Charity Majors offers blunt, pragmatic career advice for women in tech: toughen up early, stay technical to build undeniable credibility, and then use your seniority to advocate for others. She argues against leaving tech entirely, instead urging people to quit bad companies rather than the industry itself.
key_points: Early career requires endurance — focus on leveling up skills and negotiating for more money and power
Technical excellence is the most durable form of credibility, especially when your competence gets questioned repeatedly
Senior contributors should use their power to advocate for others, exploiting the research-backed loophole that women aren't punished for advocating on behalf of others
Don't quit tech, quit your job — bad companies don't deserve your talent
The rest of the world is at least as sexist as tech but without the compensation and opportunities
money_quotes: Technical excellence is currency in this industry, even more so if your credibility is gonna get challenged again and again. So don't stop engineering til you're great at it.
Don't quit tech: quit your job. There are LOTS of tolerable-to-great companies out there. If you stay and suffer, you're just rewarding the shitholes with your presence.
Learn shit, save your money, amass great power. Then use it to fuck shit up.
The research convincingly shows that women get punished for advocating for themselves, but not for advocating for others. It's a sweet loophole, use it.
key_insight: Build technical credibility and financial power first, then leverage your seniority to change the system from a position of strength rather than vulnerability.
